title: Wet-felted flower pin
subtitle: A merino brooch approximately 6 cm across, cut and shaped from a small wet-felted disc
excerpt: Wet-felt a small disc of merino roving, then cut into petal sections while damp to coax the disc into a layered flower shape. Once dry, stitch a brooch back in place. A compact first felting project that uses very little wool and can be completed in a single sitting.
sourceNotes: Observation-led; wet-felted flower brooches are a standard beginner felting project, widely used in craft workshops and fibre-arts introductory classes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres aligned in a continuous rope or strip, ready for spinning or felting. Merino roving is the standard choice for small wet-felted accessories because it produces a smooth, dense felt at a fine micron count.

The vigorous rolling and agitation stage of wet felting in which the partially matted panel is worked hard to achieve maximum shrinkage and density. For a small piece like this flower disc, fulling is done by rolling the disc in a tea towel and rubbing briskly rather than using a bamboo rolling mat.

content.text: This flower pin starts as a flat disc of
roving

wet-felted to a firm, even fabric. Cutting through from the outer edge toward the centre in evenly spaced segments and then shaping each segment upward while the disc is still damp creates the petal structure. No sewing is required until the final brooch back is attached. The disc is worked small: the pre-shrink circle is approximately 12 cm in diameter and shrinks to around 9 cm, which is then cut into petals curled to produce the 6 cm finished flower.
What you need

attrs.name: Merino roving, contrast centre colour, 3 g (optional second colour for centre)
Tea towel, for pressing and fulling
Needle and thread to stitch the brooch back

content.text: Method
Step 1: Lay the disc

Lay the felting mat on a stable work surface. Lay thin wisps of roving in a rough circle approximately 12 cm in diameter. Build up four layers in total, alternating horizontal and vertical directions. The disc does not need a perfectly sharp edge at this stage: a slightly feathered edge is fine. If adding a contrast centre colour, place a small wisp of the contrast roving in the centre of the disc on top of the final layer, covering approximately 3 cm diameter. Press it flat with your palm.
Step 2: Wet out and felt

Drizzle hot soapy water over the disc, working from the centre outward. Press down with a flat palm to saturate without displacing the circle shape. Rub with a soapy palm in small circles across the whole surface for 3 to 4 minutes. The disc will shrink and the fibres will mat together. After 3 minutes, pick up the disc and rub it between both palms: this gives more even pressure than rubbing on the mat for a small piece. Continue until the disc is firm, shows no loose surface fibres, and has shrunk to approximately 9 cm in diameter.
Step 3: Full in the tea towel
Place the damp disc in the centre of a clean tea towel. Fold the towel loosely around it and rub the bundle briskly back and forth across the work surface for 2 minutes. This is the
fulling

stage for this small piece. Unwrap and check: the disc should feel dense and uniform with no soft patches. Return to palm-rubbing with hot soapy water if any soft areas remain. The goal is a disc that holds its shape when placed flat and does not flex easily in the centre.
Step 4: Cut the petal sections

Rinse the disc once in lukewarm water and press out the excess in the tea towel. The disc should be damp but not dripping: damp felt cuts cleanly and shapes well; dry felt is harder to curl. Place the disc flat and use sharp scissors to cut from the outer edge toward the centre, stopping approximately 1.5 cm from the centre. Space the cuts evenly to create 7 or 8 petal sections. For a rounded petal shape, cut with a slight curve on each side of the segment; for a pointed petal, cut to a slight V at the tip. All petals should reach the same distance from the centre.
Step 5: Shape the petals and dry

While the disc is still damp, curl each petal section slightly upward by pressing the outer tip up with your thumb and finger. The centre of the flower stays flat against a surface while the petals curve away from it. To hold the curl while drying, place the flower on a folded cloth or the inside of a cup: the concave base keeps the petals angled at approximately 30 to 40 degrees from the centre. Allow to dry completely in this position, which takes 2 to 4 hours at room temperature. Do not dry with heat: wool felt can stiffen unevenly if dried too quickly.
Step 6: Attach the brooch back

Once the flower is completely dry and holding its shape, stitch the brooch back to the centre of the flat underside using a blunt darning needle and a short length of matching thread. Work 6 to 8 stitches through the felt around the brooch back bar mounts, passing through to just below the surface of the visible side. Tie off securely and trim the thread ends. Press the flower front gently to confirm the petals are still curved. If a petal has dried flat, dampen it briefly with warm water, re-curl with your fingers, and allow to dry again.
Variations

A second, smaller flower made from a 7 cm disc and placed on top of the first creates a layered double-flower pin. Cut the smaller disc to 5 petals instead of 7 and offset the petal positions so the layers alternate. Stitch both layers together at the centre before attaching the brooch back. A small glass bead or button sewn to the centre of the top layer completes the look.
Common problems

attrs.problem: The disc has an irregular edge rather than a circular outline
attrs.cause: The roving wisps were laid in a square or rectangular shape before wetting, and the edges felted at different rates
attrs.fix: Trim the dry or damp disc to a circle with scissors before cutting the petal sections. A small round object used as a cutting template (a jar lid, for example) pressed onto the surface and cut around gives a clean circle.

attrs.problem: The petals dried flat rather than holding the curled position
attrs.cause: The disc was shaped while too dry, or it dried on a flat surface instead of in a curved support
attrs.fix: Dampen the dried petals with a small amount of warm water, re-curl each one between finger and thumb, and place the flower in the concave support again. The felt will re-set to the new position as it dries.

attrs.problem: The disc tore at the centre when the petal cuts were made
attrs.cause: The disc was not felted sufficiently before cutting, so the centre was still loose and weak
attrs.fix: Full the disc longer before cutting. The centre should feel as dense as the outer area: rub between palms until no soft spot is detectable. If the tear is minor, press the torn fibres together while damp and allow to dry under light finger pressure.
